metadata.scope: Concise worked examples adapted from the supplied textbook concepts.
metadata.authority: Textbook-first; examples are newly worded and mathematically checked.

examples.title: Link a binary choice to production
examples.prompt: A line can produce at most 80 units, but production must be zero unless the line is opened. Opening costs 200 and each unit contributes 5. Formulate the decision.
steps: Let x be production and let y equal one exactly when the line is opened.
Link the continuous quantity to the binary choice. Substituting y=0 must force x=0; y=1 may permit the full capacity.
Subtract the fixed opening cost in the objective.
examples.finalAnswer: The binary meaning is enforced by the linking constraint, not by prose alone.

examples.title: Verify an unbounded ray
examples.prompt: Show that the SEF maximization problem is unbounded by giving a feasible base point and improving direction.
steps: Choose a feasible point and check the equality and nonnegativity.
Choose a nonnegative direction that preserves the equality.
Check that the direction strictly improves the maximization objective.
Conclude that the entire ray remains feasible and its value tends to infinity.
examples.finalAnswer: The pair (x-bar,d) is a certificate of unboundedness.

examples.title: Convert a free-variable minimization to SEF
examples.prompt: Convert the model to the course standard equality form.
steps: Replace the free variable by the difference of two nonnegative variables.
Negate the minimization objective to obtain a maximization objective.
Subtract a nonnegative slack variable from the ≥ constraint.
Check that every remaining constraint is an equality and every variable is nonnegative.
examples.finalAnswer: The transformed LP is equivalent to the original and is in SEF.

examples.title: Choose entering and leaving variables
examples.prompt: Starting from the slack basis, perform the ratio-test part of one simplex iteration.
steps: The slack basis gives the initial basic feasible solution.
Choose x1 to enter because its objective coefficient is positive.
Apply the minimum-ratio test using only positive entries in the entering column.
Increase x1 to the limiting value and read the new basic feasible solution.
examples.finalAnswer: x1 enters and x4 leaves; the objective increases from 0 to 15/2.

examples.title: Build a Phase I auxiliary LP
examples.prompt: Find a feasible solution by constructing an auxiliary LP.
steps: Make every right-hand side nonnegative.
Add one auxiliary variable to each equality to create an identity basis.
Maximize the negative sum of the auxiliary variables.
Drive the auxiliary variables to zero and recover a feasible point of the original LP.
examples.finalAnswer: Phase I attains zero, so the original LP is feasible and Phase II can start from x=(1,2).

examples.title: Recognize an extreme point by tight-constraint rank
examples.prompt: Use the tight constraints to decide whether the candidate is an extreme point.
steps: Write the inequalities in Ax≤b form.
Identify the constraints that hold with equality at the candidate.
Compute the rank of the tight normal vectors.
Compare the rank with the ambient dimension.
examples.finalAnswer: The tight normals span R^2, so the candidate is an extreme point.
examples.finalAnswerLatex: \bar x=(0,4)^{\mathsf T}\text{ is an extreme point of }P

examples.title: Construct a dual and match objective values
examples.prompt: Write the dual of the primal maximization LP and verify a matching feasible pair.
steps: Create one nonnegative dual variable for each primal ≤ constraint.
Create one ≥ dual constraint for each nonnegative primal variable.
Check primal and dual feasibility of the candidate pair.
Compare their objective values.
examples.finalAnswer: Weak duality plus equal feasible values proves both candidates optimal.

examples.title: Check complementary slackness
examples.prompt: Verify complementary slackness for the feasible primal–dual pair from the preceding duality example.
steps: Because both primal variables are positive, both corresponding dual constraints must be tight.
Because both dual variables are positive, both corresponding primal constraints must be tight.
Evaluate the four equalities.
Use the complementary-slackness theorem after feasibility has been checked.
stepsLatex: A^{\mathsf T}\bar y=(3,2)^{\mathsf T}=c,\qquad A\bar x=(4,5)^{\mathsf T}=b
examples.finalAnswer: All CS products vanish, so both feasible candidates are optimal.
examples.finalAnswerLatex: \bar x\text{ and }\bar y\text{ satisfy complementary slackness}

examples.title: Derive a valid cut from integrality
examples.prompt: Solve the LP relaxation conceptually and derive a valid inequality that removes its fractional optimum.
steps: Drop integrality and intersect the two active constraint boundaries.
Add the two inequalities to bound three times the integer objective sum.
Use integrality to round the implied right-hand side down.
Verify that the fractional LP optimum violates the new inequality while every integer-feasible point satisfies it.
examples.finalAnswer: The cut x1+x2≤2 is valid for the IP and removes the fractional LP optimum.

examples.title: Certify a convex optimum with KKT
examples.prompt: Use the course KKT condition to prove the candidate optimal.
steps: Check convexity, differentiability, and candidate feasibility.
Exhibit a Slater point satisfying the inequality strictly.
Identify the tight constraint and compute its gradient at the candidate.
Express the negative objective vector as a nonnegative multiple of that tight gradient.
examples.finalAnswer: All KKT assumptions and the cone condition hold, so the candidate is globally optimal.

examples.title: Know when simplex is finished
examples.prompt: A bakery has already performed a few pivots. Use the current canonical form to decide whether another pivot can improve profit.
steps: Start with the nonbasic variables x3 and x4 equal to zero.
Read the current basic solution and its profit directly from the canonical equations.
Look at what happens if either nonbasic variable increases.
Because every allowed increase can only lower profit, stop.
examples.finalAnswer: The current solution is optimal. In plain language: every remaining move makes the bakery earn less, so simplex should stop.

examples.title: Interpret dual variables as resource prices
examples.prompt: A workshop makes two products. Build the dual and use resource prices to prove that producing two of each product is optimal.
steps: Give each resource constraint a nonnegative price: y1 for the shared capacity and y2 for the x1 limit.
Require the priced resources used by each product to cover that product's profit.
Check that producing x=(2,2) is feasible and earns 10.
Check that prices y=(2,1) are dual feasible and value all available resources at 10.
examples.finalAnswer: The production plan and the resource-price plan meet at value 10. Weak duality says neither side can do better, so both are optimal.

examples.title: See why an unused resource has zero dual price
examples.prompt: At the proposed optimum, the third capacity has one unused unit. Use complementary slackness to explain why its dual price is zero.
steps: Evaluate the constraints at x=(2,2). The first two are full, while the third has one unit left.
Use y=(2,1,0) for the three resource prices.
Multiply each resource price by its unused amount.
Because both products are made in positive amounts, also check that both dual constraints are tight.
examples.finalAnswer: The unused third capacity has price zero. If one more unit is already unnecessary, it should not improve the optimal value.

examples.title: Cut off a half-box solution
examples.prompt: A warehouse has 5 kg of material. Every complete box uses 2 kg. The LP relaxation allows 2.5 boxes; derive the integer cut.
steps: First ignore integrality. Dividing the capacity constraint by two allows a total of at most 2.5 boxes.
Remember that the total number of complete boxes is an integer.
Round the upper bound down from 2.5 to 2.
The new inequality removes fractional totals but keeps every feasible whole-box plan.
examples.finalAnswer: Add x1+x2≤2. It says the obvious real-world fact: 5 kg cannot make three complete 2 kg boxes.

examples.title: Maximize safe machine output with KKT
examples.prompt: A machine's output is x. Minimizing -x means we want as much output as possible, but the safety rule is x²≤4. Verify that x=2 is globally optimal.
steps: Translate the model: a smaller value of -x means a larger output x.
Check the assumptions. The safety function is convex and differentiable, and x=0 is strictly safe.
At x=2 the safety constraint is tight, and its gradient points outward with value 4.
Show that the direction opposing the objective is a nonnegative multiple of that tight gradient.
examples.finalAnswer: KKT certifies x=2. In everyday terms: output cannot exceed 2 without breaking the safety limit.

examples.title: Plan standard and premium gift bags
examples.prompt: A fundraiser earns $4 per standard gift bag and $7 per premium bag. Standard bags use one packing unit, premium bags use two, 40 units are available, and at most 12 premium bags can be sold. Formulate the LP.
steps: Choose variables with units: s is the number of standard bags and p is the number of premium bags.
Turn earnings into the objective: each standard bag adds $4 and each premium bag adds $7.
Turn the packing and demand limits into inequalities.
Add nonnegativity because a negative number of bags has no real meaning.
stepsLatex: s=\#\text{ standard bags},\qquad p=\#\text{ premium bags}
examples.finalAnswer: The model chooses bag counts, maximizes fundraising earnings, and respects packing capacity and premium demand.

examples.title: Split battery load to reduce heat
examples.prompt: Two battery modules must supply a total load of 10. Heat loss in each module is the square of its load. Classify and solve the model.
steps: The squared terms make the objective quadratic, so this is not an LP.
At the minimum we use exactly the required total load; extra load would only increase heat.
For a fixed total, unequal loads add the nonnegative penalty (x1-x2)².
Therefore the lowest-heat choice splits the load evenly.
examples.finalAnswer: This is a convex quadratic optimization problem. The practical lesson is simple: sharing the load evenly minimizes heat.

examples.title: Spot an impossible staffing requirement
examples.prompt: A schedule says at least 5 workers are required but at most 3 are available. Prove the model is infeasible.
steps: Rewrite the lower bound with the same less-than-or-equal direction as the upper bound.
Add the two valid inequalities.
The variable cancels and leaves an impossible numerical statement.
That contradiction is a short certificate that no x can satisfy both rules.
examples.finalAnswer: The schedule is infeasible: one number cannot be at least 5 and at most 3.

examples.title: Turn unused workshop time into a slack variable
examples.prompt: Two products use a shared 8-hour workshop capacity. Convert the capacity inequality to standard equality form and interpret the new variable.
steps: The left side is the number of hours used.
Introduce a nonnegative variable s for hours that are not used.
Used hours plus unused hours must equal the 8 hours available.
Read s directly: s=0 means the workshop is full; s>0 means some time is left.
examples.finalAnswer: Add s≥0 and write 2x1+x2+s=8. The slack variable is simply unused workshop time.

examples.title: Use Phase I to detect two conflicting balances
examples.prompt: The original equations require the same nonnegative variable to equal both 1 and 2. Build Phase I and explain its nonzero optimum.
steps: Add one auxiliary variable to each equation so an obvious starting basis exists.
Use the course objective that maximizes the negative sum of auxiliary variables.
Auxiliary feasibility forces x1≤1, and the best choice is x1=1.
One auxiliary variable still equals 1, so the Phase I optimum is negative rather than zero.
examples.finalAnswer: Phase I cannot drive every auxiliary variable to zero, so the original equations are infeasible.

examples.title: See a corner in a storage plan
examples.prompt: A storage plan chooses amounts x1 and x2 inside a 3-by-2 rectangle. Compare the corner (3,2) with the interior point (1,1).
steps: At (3,2), the two upper-bound constraints are tight.
Their normal vectors point in independent coordinate directions, so together they pin down one point.
At (1,1), none of the four boundary constraints is tight.
The interior point can move a little in many directions, so it is not a corner.
examples.finalAnswer: (3,2) is an extreme point; (1,1) is not. Corners are pinned down by enough independent tight boundaries.
